summary: Test_topBandLayout is a meaningful, gocui-free table test that covers all four required TDD-anchor sub-cases (compact, verbose, height-guard, boundary on both sides). Every returned coordinate plus the expanded flag is asserted with named messages, mirroring the visibleColumns precedent. The guard threshold (maxY>=13) is verified at both adjacent values (12 fallback / 13 expand), which is exactly the litmus-protected behavior. Only minor, optional hardening gaps remain (no explicit zero/negative maxY case, and the verbose-fallback path is the SAME compact tuple as the unconditional compact case, so a single boundary asymmetry is theoretically possible but already pinned by the +1/-1 boundary pair).

findings.issue: No sub-case exercises pathological maxY values: maxY=0 and negative maxY. layout() in ui.go returns early on maxX==0||maxY==0 (ui.go:113) so topBandLayout is never called with maxY==0 in production, but the function itself is public-to-package and the task's Edge cases section explicitly calls out 'very short terminal (maxY small) ... never return coordinates that overlap, invert (y0>y1), or push dbstat off-screen'. The current guard (dbstatY0+2 > maxY-1) does fall back correctly for maxY<=0, but that safety is asserted nowhere — a regression that flipped the comparison would still pass all five existing rows.
findings.recommendation: Add a row e.g. {name:"verbose-zero-maxY", verbose:true, maxY:0, sysstatY1:4,pgstatY1:4,cmdlineY0:3,cmdlineY1:5,dbstatY0:4, expanded:false} (and optionally maxY:-5) to pin that the guard degrades to compact for non-positive maxY rather than emitting inverted/negative-clearing coords. This is the litmus guard for the 'never broken coords' acceptance criterion.

findings.issue: The 'height-guard' (maxY=12) and 'boundary-fallback' (maxY=12) rows assert byte-identical inputs and outputs. They are the same test executed twice. The boundary intent (the threshold flips at 13) is fully carried by the boundary-expands(13)/boundary-fallback(12) pair; the separate height-guard(12) row catches no failure that boundary-fallback(12) does not.
findings.recommendation: Either remove the redundant 'height-guard' row, or repurpose it to a DIFFERENT short value (e.g. maxY=5, a realistically tiny terminal well below the threshold) so it covers a distinct point on the input range instead of duplicating maxY=12. Keeping a distinct small value also partially addresses the pathological-maxY gap above.
